ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP for legume-based snack food is the closest parent; no specific masala vadai entry exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for masala vadai or an adequately close parent concept in the food hierarchy.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of masala vadai consists of 2 pieces, each approximately 50 g, totalling approximately 100 g.

nutrient_source.title: Paruppu Vadai (Chana Dal Vada) — Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition) reports values for paruppu vadai / chana dal vada, which is the base preparation of masala vadai. Values are adapted for the standard deep-fried chana dal fritter. Masala vadai is the spiced variant of paruppu vadai; macronutrient profile is consistent with the ICMR-NIN entry for deep-fried chana dal fritters.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Chickpea / chana dal fritter analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for masala vadai. GI estimated at 35 based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for chickpea-based foods and chana dal preparations (GI range 28–45 for legume-based fritters and cooked chana dal). Deep-frying and the high protein and fibre content of chana dal are known to lower glycemic response. GI of 35 is a conservative mid-range estimate consistent with published values for similar legume fritters. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (35 × 35.0 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 100 g serving) / 100 = 12.25, rounded to 12.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~320 mg per 100 g) and deep-frying
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit masala vadai intake to one piece per occasion, as the sodium content from salt and spice blends combined with the oil absorbed during deep-frying can contribute meaningfully to daily sodium targets of under 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~35) due to high legume fibre and protein content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Masala vadai has a low estimated glycemic index of approximately 35, driven by the high fibre and protein content of chana dal, making it a relatively suitable snack for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in a standard two-piece portion and paired with a low-GI meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat content (~16 g per 100 g) from deep-frying, though saturated fat is relatively low (~2 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While masala vadai contains predominantly unsaturated fats and zero cholesterol, the total fat load from deep-frying (~16 g per 100 g) warrants moderation for individuals with cardiovascular disease; choosing air-fried or baked variants and limiting portion size to two pieces can reduce cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Masala Vadai (also known as Paruppu Vadai or Chana Dal Vada) is a popular South Indian deep-fried snack made primarily from coarsely ground chana dal (split chickpeas), seasoned with onions, green chillies, ginger, curry leaves, fennel seeds, and fresh coriander. The batter is shaped into flat, round patties and deep-fried in oil until golden brown and crispy on the outside, while remaining slightly dense and savoury on the inside. The texture is characteristically crunchy on the exterior with a hearty, slightly coarse interior. Masala vadai is widely consumed in South India, Sri Lanka, and among the Indian Tamil diaspora in Singapore and Malaysia, where it is a staple snack at Indian hawker stalls and tiffin centres. In Singapore, it is commonly served as a tea-time snack or as part of a banana-leaf meal. Nutritionally, masala vadai is a good source of plant-based protein (~13.5 g per 100 g) and dietary fibre (~5.5 g per 100 g) from chana dal, and provides meaningful amounts of iron, folate, and B vitamins. Its estimated glycemic index of approximately 35 places it in the low-GI category, making it relatively suitable for individuals managing blood glucose. However, the deep-frying process adds significant fat (~16 g per 100 g) and caloric density (~330 kcal per 100 g), and the sodium content (~320 mg per 100 g) warrants attention for those managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. A standard serving is two pieces (~100 g).
common_user_queries: How many calories in masala vadai?
Is masala vadai good for diabetics?
What is the GI of masala vadai?
What are the ingredients in masala vadai?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) for paruppu vadai / chana dal vada, which is the direct base preparation of masala vadai. The GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for chickpea-based foods, as no direct GI measurement for masala vadai was found in the approved sources. Confidence is medium rather than high because the nutrient match is adapted rather than exact.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) for paruppu vadai (chana dal vada), the base preparation of masala vadai. The spice additions (onion, chilli, ginger, curry leaves) do not materially alter the macronutrient profile. 2. GI is an analogous estimate of 35 based on University of Sydney GI Database values for chickpea and chana dal preparations; no direct GI measurement for masala vadai was identified in approved sources. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this preparation. 4. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this preparation. 5. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this preparation. 6. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this preparation. 7. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this preparation. 8. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 as masala vadai is a plant-based food with no animal-derived ingredients; confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN for plant-based legume preparations. 9. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as masala vadai contains no animal-derived ingredients; confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN for plant-based legume preparations. 10. trans_fat_g is 0.0 as traditional deep-frying in vegetable oil does not produce significant trans fats; confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN. 11. The field survey listing specifies 2 pieces at SGD 7.50 from Anjana Kitchen, Six Battery Road, Singapore. Serving size is standardised to 100 g (2 pieces × ~50 g each). 12. Glycemic load per serving = (35 × 35.0 / 100) × 100 g = 12.25, reported as 12.3.
